metadata.title: Guideline on the pharmacokinetic and clinical evaluation of modified release dosage forms

rules.rule_text: The development of a modified release formulation must be based on a well-defined clinical need and on an integration of physiological, pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic considerations. The dossier submitted in support of a marketing authorisation must provide a complete justification of the physical form of the MR device, the choice of dosage form, the choice of active substance contents, and the clinical rationale for the new dosage form.
rules.source_section: Section 1.2 Rationale for Development
rules.verbatim_quote: The development of a modified release formulation has to be based on a well-defined clinical need (e.g. improvement of patient compliance and/or safety) and on an integration of physiological, pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic considerations. The dossier submitted in support of an application for a marketing authorisation must provide a complete justification of: The physical form of the modified release device and the mechanism of the release form; The choice of the dosage form, defining the in vitro and in vivo performance of the product; The choice of active substance contents per unit of the dosage form; The clinical rationale for the new dosage form, particularly in relation to the proposed indications and posology.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: Clinical trials, including bioequivalence and pharmacokinetic studies, conducted in the EU/EEA must be carried out in accordance with Directive 2001/20/EC. Trials conducted outside the EU intended for use in a Marketing Authorisation Application in the EU/EEA must be conducted to the standards set out in Annex I of the community code, Directive 2001/83/EC as amended.
rules.source_section: Section 3. Legal basis and relevant guidelines
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical trials, including bioequivalence and pharmacokinetic studies, conducted in the EU/EEA have to be carried out in accordance with Directive 2001/20/EC. Trials conducted outside of the EU and intended for use in a Marketing Authorisation Application in the EU/EEA have to be conducted to the standards set out in Annex I of the community code, Directive 2001/83/EC as amended.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: Test products used in bioequivalence studies must be prepared in accordance with GMP regulations including Eudralex volume 4.
rules.source_section: Section 3. Legal basis and relevant guidelines
rules.verbatim_quote: The test products used in the bioequivalence study must be prepared in accordance with GMP-regulations including Eudralex volume 4.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For a new chemical entity developed first as a modified release formulation, the submitted dossier must contain the appropriate pharmaceutical and chemical data, necessary preclinical studies and a complete clinical data package as for any full application.
rules.source_section: Section 4. Applications for modified release dosage forms of new chemical entities
rules.verbatim_quote: If a new chemical entity is developed first as a modified release formulation, the submitted dossier should contain the appropriate pharmaceutical and chemical data, necessary preclinical studies and a complete clinical data package as for any full application.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: A complete pharmacokinetic data package is required for a new chemical entity developed as MR formulation. Additional documentation specific to the MR dosage form must include studies evaluating factors affecting the biopharmaceutic performance of the modified release formulation (food effect, dose dumping risk, GI function, special populations).
rules.source_section: Section 4.1 Pharmacokinetic studies required for oral MR formulation of a new chemical entity
rules.verbatim_quote: A complete pharmacokinetic data package is required for a new chemical entity developed as MR formulation. Additional documentation specific to the MR dosage form include studies evaluating factors affecting the biopharmaceutic performance of the modified release formulation (see section 5.1.4 and 5.1.5).
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: Food effect studies for new MR formulations must be conducted early during drug development so that appropriate recommendations regarding intake in relation to food can be included in clinical efficacy and safety studies. The risk for dose dumping must be evaluated before initiation of efficacy and safety studies.
rules.source_section: Section 4.1.1 Food effect studies with oral modified release forms
rules.verbatim_quote: Food effect studies for new MR formulations are recommended to be conducted early during drug development so that appropriate recommendations regarding intake in relation to food can be included in clinical efficacy and safety studies. This is also important from a safety perspective as the risk for dose dumping should be evaluated before initiation of efficacy and safety studies.
rules.exceptions: A 2-way crossover study (MR formulation fasting and fed) may be sufficient if there is no clinically relevant food effect. If there is a clinically relevant food effect, additional studies with an oral solution can be considered.

rules.rule_text: For TDDS of a new chemical entity, in-vitro and in-vivo investigations must be conducted to evaluate drug diffusion characteristics and the rate limiting step that determines systemic availability. Pharmacokinetic investigations must comprise single-dose and multiple-dose investigations considering application site-dependent absorption, fluctuation, lag-times and concentration-time profile after patch removal.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 Pharmacokinetic Studies required for Transdermal Drug Delivery Systems (TDDS) of a new chemical entity
rules.verbatim_quote: In-vitro and in-vivo investigations should be conducted to evaluate drug diffusion characteristics and the rate limiting step that determines systemic availability i.e. drug release and/or skin reservoir and/or other formulation related particularities. Pharmacokinetic investigations should comprise single-dose and multiple-dose investigations considering particular aspects like e.g. application site-dependent absorption, fluctuation, lag-times and concentration time profile after patch removal.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For TDDS of a new chemical entity, skin irritation, sensitisation, phototoxicity and patch adhesion must be investigated in addition to conventional phase I studies. The Product Information Leaflet must provide clear instructions on the use in special situations (e.g. sauna). To avoid medication errors from poor visibility, the development of invisible patches should be considered conservatively.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 Pharmacokinetic Studies required for Transdermal Drug Delivery Systems (TDDS) of a new chemical entity
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to conventional phase I studies, skin irritation, sensitisation (see also appendix I), phototoxicity (see also ICH S10 Guidance on photosafety evaluation of pharmaceuticals) and patch adhesion (see also appendix IV) should be investigated. To evaluate patch adhesion, the influence of external factors (e.g. heat, sun cream) should be considered. TDDS usually deliver drugs intended for elderly people. Therefore tests should be performed in individuals with similar skin conditions as the expected patients (see also appendix IV). The Product Information Leaflet should provide clear instructions on the use in special situations (e.g. sauna).
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For intramuscular/subcutaneous depot formulations of a new chemical entity, in-vitro and in-vivo investigations must be conducted to evaluate drug diffusion characteristics from the IM/SC depot and the rate limiting step determining systemic availability. Establishing an IVIVC is advisable.
rules.source_section: Section 4.3 Pharmacokinetic Studies required for intramuscular/subcutaneous Depot formulations of a new chemical entity
rules.verbatim_quote: The kinetics of intramuscular depot formulations is determined by the interplay between the active substance, the formulation and the muscle tissue. In-vitro and in-vivo investigations should be conducted to evaluate drug diffusion characteristics from the IM/SC depot and the rate limiting step that determines systemic availability i.e. drug release and/or other formulation related particularities. Pharmacokinetic investigations should comprise single-dose and multiple-dose investigations considering particular aspects like e.g. application site-dependent absorption, fluctuation and lag-times. Aiming to establish an IVIVC is advisable.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: When the strength of the new modified release formulation differs from those approved for the immediate release product, this difference and the possible resulting different dosage regimen must be highlighted very clearly in SmPC, PIL and labelling as most important routine risk minimisation measures to avoid medication errors. The applicant must prove that the benefits of the new formulation outweigh the potential risks linked with this product.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Application for a modified release formulation of a drug that is authorised in a formulation with a different release rate
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever the strength of the new modified release formulation differs from those approved for the immediate release product this difference and the possible resulting different dosage regimen has to be highlighted very clearly in SmPC, PIL and labelling as most important routine risk minimisation measures to avoid medication errors. The applicant has to prove that the benefits of the new formulation outweigh the potential risks (e.g. medication error) linked with this product.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: Active metabolites must be measured in pharmacokinetic studies for modified release formulations since a change in absorption rate or route of administration may modify the extent and pattern of metabolism. The metabolite requirements from the Guideline on the Investigation of Bioequivalence (CPMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98) are not applicable for MR formulations.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1 Pharmacokinetic studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to the substantial formulation impact the requirements about metabolites given in the 'Guideline on the Investigation of Bioequivalence (CPMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98)' is not applicable in this case. Active metabolites should be measured since a change in absorption rate or route of administration may modify the extent and pattern of metabolism.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: When multiple dose studies are performed it must be demonstrated that steady-state has been reached. Achievement of steady-state is assessed by comparing at least three pre-dose concentrations for each formulation, unless otherwise justified.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1 Pharmacokinetic studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever multiple dose studies are performed it should be demonstrated that steady state has been reached. Achievement of steady-state is assessed by comparing at least three pre-dose concentrations for each formulation, unless otherwise justified.
rules.exceptions: In case of no accumulation (i.e. insignificant levels at the end of the dosing interval) based on the criteria outlined in section 6.1, multiple dose studies are not required.

rules.rule_text: The influence of food on the bioavailability of oral modified release formulations must be investigated in a single dose study. The optimal experimental conditions require ingestion of a predefined high-fat high-calorie meal immediately before dosing. The meal must be a high-fat (approximately 50 percent of total caloric content) and high-calorie (approximately 800 to 1000 kcal) meal, deriving approximately 150, 250, and 500-600 kcal from protein, carbohydrate and fat respectively.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.4.1 Food
rules.verbatim_quote: The influence of food on the bioavailability of oral modified release formulations must be investigated in a single dose study. The optimal experimental conditions to produce a food effect include the ingestion of a predefined high-fat high-calorie meal immediately before dosing. It is recommended that subjects should start the meal 30 minutes prior to administration of the drug product and finish this meal within 30 minutes. The meal should be a high-fat (approximately 50 percent of total caloric content of the meal) and high-calorie (approximately 800 to 1000 kcal) meal. This test meal should derive approximately 150, 250, and 500-600 kcal from protein, carbohydrate and fat, respectively.
rules.exceptions: Whenever there are several strengths, the food effect can be investigated for one of the strengths only if the products are proportional in composition, have the same manufacturing process, exhibit linear pharmacokinetics and their dissolution profiles are similar in a range of dissolution media.

rules.rule_text: For modified release formulations, the risk for unexpected release (dose dumping) must be excluded. If dose dumping is observed or suspected, the product must be reformulated to avoid this deficiency of biopharmaceutical quality.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.4.3 Unexpected release characteristics (e.g. dose dumping)
rules.verbatim_quote: For modified release formulations the risk for unexpected release resulting in unforeseen exposure should be excluded. If dose dumping is observed (e.g. much higher peak exposure with an inadequate modified release profile) or suspected (e.g. absence of levels of a labile active substance in gastro-resistant formulation for some subjects) the product should be reformulated to avoid this deficiency of the biopharmaceutical quality.
rules.exceptions: Where the increased exposure is not related to a particular product failure causing uncontrolled release, dosing recommendations with regard to e.g. concomitant food intake should be implemented to avoid a prolonged residence in the stomach.

rules.rule_text: For modified release oral dosage forms containing active substances with higher solubility in ethanol, in vitro studies of the release in alcohol solutions must be performed. Where accelerated active substance release is seen in vitro at high or low alcohol concentrations, the product must be reformulated. Contraindication of alcohol as the sole measure to address a formulation interaction is generally not considered appropriate.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.4.3 Effects of alcohol (subsection within dose dumping)
rules.verbatim_quote: For such formulations, in vitro studies of the release in alcohol solutions should be performed. Where accelerated active substance release is seen in vitro either at high or low alcohol concentrations over a short period of time or at lower alcohol concentrations over a longer period of time, the product should be reformulated. Only in those cases where it can be justified that an in vitro alcohol interaction cannot be avoided by reformulation, an in vivo study could be accepted, in order to substantiate that such an interaction is unlikely to occur in vivo.
rules.exceptions: If the significant dose-dumping effect is likely in vivo and cannot be avoided by reformulation, the benefit/risk of the product needs to be carefully considered. Information on relevant interactions with alcohol, in case of possible clinically relevant potentiation or a harmful additive effect should be given in the product information.

rules.rule_text: For oral modified release formulations co-administered with active substances affecting gastrointestinal physiology (e.g. opioids), it is necessary to investigate the performance of the oral modified release formulation under these conditions.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.4.2 Gastro-intestinal function
rules.verbatim_quote: If an oral modified release formulation is to be usually co-administered with active substances affecting gastrointestinal physiology (e.g. opioids) it is necessary to investigate the performance of the oral modified release formulation under these conditions.
rules.exceptions: If the oral MR formulation is intended for patients with markedly altered gastrointestinal function, the MR formulation may need to be studied also in those patients.

rules.rule_text: As a principle, comparative clinical efficacy and safety data are needed in addition to PK data for modified release products developed after the immediate release formulation, unless adequately justified. The major issue is to demonstrate that the new MR formulation is as safe and effective as the existing formulation.
rules.source_section: Section 5.2 Therapeutic studies
rules.verbatim_quote: As a principle, comparative clinical efficacy and safety data are needed in addition to PK data for modified release products developed after the immediate release formulation, unless adequately justified. As the efficacy and safety of the immediate release product is known, the major issue would be to demonstrate that the new modified release formulation is as safe and effective as the existing formulation. Additional benefits of the new formulation should be shown or justified, if claimed.
rules.exceptions: In exceptional cases, if the assessment of concentration-effect relationship indicates a well-defined relationship between plasma concentration(s) and clinical response, clinical trials may be considered unnecessary. In this case the same or a better level of efficacy and safety has to be concluded from PK/PD studies.

rules.rule_text: Therapeutic studies may only be waived if at least one of the following conditions is met: bioequivalence between reference and test product is shown in terms of Cmax,ss, Cmin,ss and AUC(0-t)ss, and the new MR product is developed to actually mimic the performance of a product with a different release mechanism; OR bioequivalence is shown despite differences in the shape of the plasma concentration-time profile with justification that shape differences have no relevance for efficacy and safety; OR there is a well-defined therapeutic window, the rate of input is known not to influence the safety and efficacy profile, and specific AUC and Cmax,ss/Cmin,ss criteria are met.
rules.source_section: Section 5.2.1 Waiving of therapeutic studies
rules.verbatim_quote: In principle therapeutic studies are necessary. However, therapeutic studies might be waived in case at least one of the following conditions is met: bioequivalence between the reference and the test product is shown in terms of Cmax,ss, Cmin,ss and AUC(0-t)ss because the new modified product is developed to actually mimic the performance of product with an different release mechanism and its dosage regimen e.g. a pulsatile multiphasic release dosage form containing pellets with different lag time.
rules.exceptions: See conditions listed in Section 5.2.1 for each waiver pathway

rules.rule_text: For generic prolonged release oral formulations, bioequivalence must be demonstrated using: a single-dose fasting study, a single-dose fed study using a high-fat meal, and a multiple-dose study (unless waived based on accumulation criteria). Both test and reference formulations must be compared.
rules.source_section: Section 6.1.1 Studies generally required to demonstrate bioequivalence (Prolonged release formulations)
rules.verbatim_quote: a single-dose fasting study comparing test and reference drug product; a single-dose fed study using a high-fat meal (see 5.1.4.1) comparing test and reference drug product; a multiple-dose study comparing test and reference drug product.
rules.exceptions: A multiple dose study is not needed if a single dose study with the highest strength has demonstrated that the mean AUC(0-t) after the first dose covers more than 90% of mean AUC(0-inf) for both test and reference, and consequently a low extent of accumulation is expected.

rules.rule_text: In steady-state bioequivalence studies, the washout period of the previous treatment can overlap with the build-up of the second treatment (direct switching), provided the build-up period is sufficiently long (at least 5 times the terminal half-life). Whether steady-state has been achieved is assessed by comparing at least three pre-dose concentrations for each formulation.
rules.source_section: Section 6.1.1.2 Multiple dose studies
rules.verbatim_quote: In steady-state studies, the washout period of the previous treatment can overlap with the build-up of the second treatment (direct switching), provided the build-up period is sufficiently long (at least 5 times the terminal half-life). Whether the steady-state has been achieved is assessed by comparing at least three pre-dose concentrations for each formulation.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For bioequivalence studies of delayed release formulations, the same fasting and fed study requirements apply as for prolonged release. However, for delayed release there is in principle no need for multiple dose studies except when single dose studies cannot be performed because of safety concerns.
rules.source_section: Section 6.2.1 Studies generally required to demonstrate bioequivalence (Delayed release)
rules.verbatim_quote: a single-dose fasting study comparing test and reference product; a single-dose fed study using a high-fat meal (see 5.1.4.1) comparing test and reference product. A similar approach as detailed for prolonged release forms regarding study design of single dose studies can be used (see 6.1.1.1).
rules.exceptions: In principle there is no need for multiple dose studies except when single dose studies cannot be performed because of safety concerns.

rules.rule_text: For bioequivalence of prolonged release products with accumulation, the following parameters must be evaluated statistically: Single dose studies — AUC(0-t), AUC(0-inf), Cmax; Multiple dose studies — AUC(0-t), Cmax,ss, Ct,ss. A truncated AUC(0-72h) is not acceptable for MR products.
rules.source_section: Section 6.8.1 Parameters to be analysed / Section 6.8.2.1 Parameters to be evaluated
rules.verbatim_quote: In studies to determine bioequivalence after a single dose, AUC(0-t), AUC(0-∞), residual area, Cmax, tmax should be determined and, when relevant, partialAUC. A truncated AUC(0-72h) is not acceptable for MR products.
rules.exceptions: For prolonged release products with no risk of accumulation, a representative metric of the shape of the curve (e.g. early and terminal partialAUCs) must also be included.

rules.rule_text: The standard bioequivalence acceptance limits of 80.00-125.00% apply for generic MR products. Any widening of the acceptance criteria for Cmax must follow the recommendations for highly variable drug products in the Guideline on the Investigation of Bioequivalence (CPMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98).
rules.source_section: Section 6.8.2.2 Statistical evaluation and acceptance criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: The bioequivalence approach considering usual acceptance limits (80.00 – 125.00 %) is applicable for generic MR products (see CPMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98). Any widening of the acceptance criteria for Cmax should follow the recommendations for highly variable drug products in the Guideline on the Investigation of Bioequivalence (CPMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98).
rules.exceptions: Widening for highly variable drugs allowed per CPMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98; similar widening can be applied for Cmax,ss, Ct,ss and partialAUC.

rules.rule_text: For delayed and multiphasic release formulations, differences in tmax must also be assessed. There should be no apparent difference in median tmax and its range between test and reference product. A formal statistical evaluation of tmax is not required.
rules.source_section: Section 6.8.2.2 Statistical evaluation and acceptance criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: For delayed and multphasic release formulations differences in tmax is also recommended to be assessed, especially for products where a fast onset of action is important. A formal statistical evaluation of tmax is not required. However, there should be no apparent difference in median tmax and its range between test and reference product.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For generic oral MR formulations, in vitro studies of release in alcohol solutions must be performed. Where accelerated active substance release is seen in vitro (at high or low alcohol concentrations over a short or longer period of time), the product must be reformulated.
rules.source_section: Section 6.9 Effects of alcohol
rules.verbatim_quote: For generic oral formulations, in vitro studies of the release in alcohol solutions should be performed. Where accelerated active substance release is seen in vitro either at high or low alcohol concentrations over a short period of time or at lower alcohol concentrations over a longer period of time, the product should be reformulated.
rules.exceptions: If the alcohol effect cannot be avoided and it is present also in the reference product, the applicant should justify/demonstrate that it lacks clinical relevance or discuss the possible clinical relevance in comparison to the reference product.

rules.rule_text: A generic TDDS must demonstrate both comparable or better adhesion properties (meeting the 90% CI adhesion threshold) and bioequivalence. Equivalence testing of TDDS must comprise both comparable or better adhesion properties and bioequivalence. It is advisable to ensure comparable or better adhesion properties prior to bioequivalence investigations, since inferior adhesion could invalidate the pharmacokinetic results.
rules.source_section: Section 6.5 Transdermal Drug Delivery Systems (TDDS)
rules.verbatim_quote: Equivalence testing of TDDS should comprise both comparable or better adhesion properties (see appendix IV) and bioequivalence. It is advisable to ensure comparable or better adhesion properties prior to bioequivalence investigations in volunteers since inferior adhesion could invalidate the pharmacokinetic results and question the acceptability of the product.
rules.exceptions: Unless otherwise justified by e.g. very similar quantitative and qualitative composition.

rules.rule_text: For TDDS equivalence, the test product must demonstrate similar or lower degree of local irritation, phototoxicity and sensitisation, and similar or better adhesiveness to the skin as the reference product. Comparative state-of-the-art studies are required to investigate cutaneous tolerability, irritation and sensitisation, potential phototoxicity, and adhesion characteristics.
rules.source_section: Section 6.5 Transdermal Drug Delivery Systems (TDDS)
rules.verbatim_quote: The test product should demonstrate a similar or lower degree of local irritation, phototoxicity, sensitization, and similar or better adhesiveness to the skin as the reference product. In order to ensure equivalence in terms of safety, comparative state-of-the-art studies are required to investigate cutaneous tolerability, irritation and sensitisation (see appendix I); the potential to produce phototoxic reactions; adhesion characteristics (for details regarding comparative adhesion tests see appendix IV) unless otherwise justified by e.g. very similar quantitative and qualitative composition.
rules.exceptions: Unless otherwise justified by very similar quantitative and qualitative composition.

rules.rule_text: For TDDS in vivo adhesion studies, the 90% confidence interval of mean adherence for the test product at the end of the dosing interval must lie above 90%. Any deviation from this requirement must be justified considering all potential risks associated with the incomplete attachment of the patch.
rules.source_section: Appendix II: in vivo skin adhesion - Assessment criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, it is expected that the 90% confidence interval of mean adherence for the test product at the end of the dosing interval should lie above 90%. This should therefore normally be the primary comparison. Any deviation from this requirement has to be justified considering all potential risks associated with the incomplete attachment of the patch.
rules.exceptions: If the reference product has poor adherence (< 90%), non-inferiority may be established: the lower limit of the 90% CI for the difference of adhesiveness (test – reference) should not be less than -10%.

rules.rule_text: When a modified release formulation is developed, it is highly recommended to establish an in vitro in vivo correlation (IVIVC) to quantify in vivo release and formulation-related effect on absorption, to establish the in vivo relevance of in vitro dissolution tests, and to support biowaiver claims. Where an IVIVC is used to support a biowaiver, a validated Level A correlation is generally a prerequisite.
rules.source_section: Appendix III: in vitro in vivo correlation - Section 1 Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: When a modified release formulation is developed, it is highly recommended to establish an IVIVC: a) to quantify in vivo release and formulation related effect on absorption, b) to establish the in vivo relevance of in vitro dissolution tests and associated dissolution specifications c) to support biowaiver claims in later phases of clinical development or post-authorisation if there are changes in formulation. Level A IVIVCs, in contrast to levels B and C, predict the entire concentration-time profile and for this reason are highly encouraged. Where an IVIVC is used to support a biowaiver, a validated level A correlation is generally a prerequisite.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For IVIVC model qualification, the absolute value of the prediction error for all summary parameters must be less than 15% for each formulation, and the average prediction error must be less than 10% for each summary parameter. For external validation, the absolute value of the prediction error must be less than 10% for each formulation.
rules.source_section: Appendix III: Section 3.4 IVIVC Model Qualification and Predictability Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: The absolute value of the prediction error for all summary parameters should be less than 15% for each formulation and the average prediction error for all formulations included in IVIVC development should be less than 10% for each summary parameter. The absolute value of the prediction error for all summary parameters should be less than 10% for each formulation used for external validation.
rules.exceptions: If an individual formulation is inadequately predicted, it is acceptable to redevelop the IVIVC excluding the outlier formulation, but at least two formulations must remain and the exclusion should be supported by discussion of possible reasons.

rules.rule_text: For transdermal patches, skin irritation and sensitisation equivalence must be assessed using a formal non-inferiority statistical approach. The 90% confidence interval of the ratio of least squares means of the test treatment to reference treatment must fall completely within the interval 0.8 to 1.25 to conclude equivalence for irritation scores.
rules.source_section: Appendix I: sensitisation and irritation test for transdermal products
rules.verbatim_quote: The ratio of the least squares means of the test treatment to the reference treatment has to be calculated, along with its 90% confidence interval. A 90% confidence interval that falls completely within the interval 0.8 to 1.25 leads to the conclusion that the two treatments are equivalent.
rules.exceptions: None stated

rules.rule_text: For multiphasic modified release products, bioequivalence must be demonstrated for additional pharmacokinetic parameters covering all phases. Single dose parameters must include partialAUC and Cmax in all phases. The time point for truncating the partialAUC must be based on the PK profile and must be justified and pre-specified in the study protocol.
rules.source_section: Section 6.8.1 Parameters to be analysed / Section 6.8.2.1 Parameters to be evaluated
rules.verbatim_quote: For multiphatic modified release products additional parameters to be determined include partialAUC, Cmax and tmax in all phases. The time point for truncating the partialAUC should be based on the PK profile for the e.g. IR and the MR parts respectively and should be justified and pre-specified in the study protocol.
rules.exceptions: None stated
